content.text: Organizing Resources

Azure resources exist within a four-level scope hierarchy: management group > subscription > resource group > resource. Each level serves a distinct organizational purpose and is the attachment point for Policy and RBAC assignments, which inherit downward. Understanding which level to use for each organizational concern is the foundation of a manageable Azure environment.

attrs.alt: Azure resource organization hierarchy — from tenant root to individual resources

content.text: Resource groups are the primary unit of organization for resources with the same lifecycle. If two resources are deployed together, managed together, and deleted together, they belong in the same resource group.
The Four Scope Levels and Their Purpose
Scope level
Primary purpose
RBAC / Policy scope?
Billing boundary?
Management group
Group subscriptions for Policy and RBAC that span multiple subscriptions
Yes — inherits to all child subscriptions
No
Subscription
Billing unit; resource limits (cores, IPs) are per-subscription; blast radius containment
Yes — inherits to all child resource groups
Yes
Resource group
Lifecycle grouping; deployment unit; access control for a workload
Yes — inherits to all child resources
No (costs aggregate to subscription)
Resource
The actual service instance (VM, storage account, database, etc.)
Yes — most granular RBAC scope
Costs roll up to subscription
Resource Group Design Patterns

The most common resource group patterns are: one RG per workload (all resources for an application in one RG), one RG per layer (separate app/data/network RGs per workload), one RG per environment (all dev resources in one RG), and one RG per team (team-owned resources together). The layer pattern (app + data + network) is recommended for production workloads because it matches both lifecycle and access control requirements.
Pattern
Structure
Best for
Drawbacks
Per-workload
rg-payments-prod-eastus (all layers together)
Small workloads, single-team ownership
Mixing compute and data complicates access control
Per-layer (recommended)
rg-payments-app-prod, rg-payments-data-prod, rg-payments-network-prod
Workloads with separate app/DBA/network teams
More RGs to manage; cross-RG references needed
Per-environment
rg-dev-eastus (all dev apps), rg-prod-eastus (all prod)
Very small orgs with one team across all apps
Blast radius: deleting one RG removes everything in that env
Per-team
rg-payments-team, rg-catalog-team
Team-based billing and access control
Breaks lifecycle grouping when a team maintains multiple environments
Creating and Managing Resource Groups

Resource groups are lightweight ARM resources in the Microsoft.Resources/resourceGroups namespace. Creating one is a prerequisite for all other deployments. Apply tags at creation time — they can be updated later but establishing them early prevents drift. Use az resource move to relocate individual resources between groups within the same subscription.

# Creating and managing resource groups

# Create a resource group following the naming convention
az group create \
  --name rg-payments-prod-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=prod Application=payments CostCenter=cc-123 Owner=payments-team@contoso.com

# List all resource groups in the current subscription
az group list --query '[].{Name:name, Location:location, State:properties.provisioningState}' -o table

# Move resources between resource groups
# Moving is non-destructive but some resource types cannot be moved
az resource move \
  --destination-group rg-payments-prod-eastus \
  --ids \
    '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-old/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-payments-dev-eastus-001' \
    '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-old/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-payments-dev-eastus'

# Lock a resource group to prevent accidental deletion
az lock create \
  --name 'rg-delete-lock' \
  --resource-group rg-payments-prod-eastus \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete

# Delete a resource group and ALL resources inside it (irreversible)
# az group delete --name rg-payments-dev-eastus --yes --no-wait
Subscription Organization Patterns

Each Azure subscription is an independent billing unit with its own resource quotas (vCPU limits, public IP limits, storage account limits). The Cloud Adoption Framework recommends separating major workloads into their own subscriptions to contain blast radius, isolate billing, and avoid hitting per-subscription limits in large environments. Subscriptions are then grouped into management groups for unified Policy and RBAC governance.

# Subscription-level organization patterns

# List all subscriptions accessible to the current user
az account list --query '[].{Name:name, Id:id, State:state}' -o table

# Set the active subscription for subsequent CLI commands
az account set --subscription 'sub-payments-prod'

# Create a management group (requires tenant-level permissions)
az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-workloads' \
  --display-name 'Workloads'

# Move a subscription into a management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name 'mg-workloads' \
  --subscription '<subscription-id>'

# Subscription-per-workload pattern:
# Each major workload gets its own subscription — this is the recommended CAF pattern.
# Benefits: billing isolation, blast radius containment, independent subscription limits.
# Typical layout:
#   sub-connectivity-prod   -> hub VNet, Firewall, DNS, ExpressRoute
#   sub-identity-prod       -> AD DS, Azure AD DS, certificates
#   sub-payments-prod       -> payments application resources
#   sub-catalog-prod        -> catalog application resources
#   sub-shared-services-prod -> ACR, monitoring, Key Vault shared services
#   sub-dev-sandbox         -> developer experimentation (auto-delete policy)
Creating Resource Groups via Bicep

When deploying infrastructure as code, create resource groups at subscription scope (targetScope = 'subscription') before deploying resources into them. A single subscription-scope Bicep file can create multiple resource groups (app, data, network) and output their names for consumption by downstream module deployments. This approach ensures resource groups are part of the IaC state rather than created manually.
Creating Resource Groups with Terraform

Terraform's azurerm_resource_group resource maps directly to the ARM resource group. The best pattern is a reusable module that creates the app, data, and network resource groups for a workload in one call, outputting the names for downstream consumption. Add a validation block on the environment variable to catch typos before az plan runs.
Moving Resources Between Resource Groups and Subscriptions

Azure supports moving most resource types between resource groups and between subscriptions using az resource move or the ARM validateMoveResources action. Before moving, validate that the target resource types are moveable — some types (resources with private endpoints, Backup vault items, some network resources) cannot be moved and must be recreated. Move operations are transient: they take resources offline briefly for some types, so schedule them during a maintenance window.

# Moving resources between subscriptions and resource groups

# Step 1: Validate that the resources can be moved
# (Some types cannot be moved — check before attempting)
az resource invoke-action \
  --action validateMoveResources \
  --ids '/subscriptions/<src-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-old' \
  --request-body '{
    "resources": [
      "/subscriptions/<src-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-old/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-payments-dev-eastus-001",
      "/subscriptions/<src-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-old/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-old"
    ],
    "targetResourceGroup": "/subscriptions/<dst-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-payments-prod-eastus"
  }'

# Step 2: Actually move the resources
az resource move \
  --destination-group rg-payments-prod-eastus \
  --destination-subscription-id '<dst-sub-id>' \
  --ids \
    '/subscriptions/<src-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-old/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-payments-dev-eastus-001' \
    '/subscriptions/<src-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-old/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-old'

# Note: The move operation takes the resources offline momentarily for some types.
# Disks, NICs, and other VM dependencies must be moved together with the VM.

# Resource types that CANNOT be moved cross-subscription (common examples):
#   - Azure Active Directory resources
#   - Azure DevOps projects
#   - Azure Backup vaults with active backup items
#   - Express Route circuits
#   - Most resources with private endpoints (endpoint must be deleted first)
Enforcing Resource Group Organization with Policy

Azure Policy can require that every resource group carries mandatory organizational tags (CostCenter, Environment) using a Deny effect at the management group scope. This gates resource group creation rather than trying to remediate tags after the fact. Pair the Policy with a Resource Graph query to audit existing non-compliant resource groups and produce a backlog for manual remediation.

# Policy: require all resource groups to have mandatory tags

# 1. Policy definition: deny RG creation without required tags
az policy definition create \
  --name 'require-rg-tags' \
  --display-name 'Require CostCenter and Environment tags on resource groups' \
  --mode All \
  --rules '{
    "if": {
      "allOf": [
        {"field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.Resources/resourceGroups"},
        {"anyOf": [
          {"field": "tags[CostCenter]",   "exists": false},
          {"field": "tags[Environment]",  "exists": false}
        ]}
      ]
    },
    "then": {"effect": "Deny"}
  }' \
  --management-group mg-corp

# 2. Assign the policy at the management group level
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'require-rg-tags' \
  --policy 'require-rg-tags' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

# 3. Audit existing non-compliant resource groups with Resource Graph
az graph query -q "
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| where isnull(tags['CostCenter']) or isnull(tags['Environment'])
| project name, subscriptionId, location
| order by subscriptionId asc, name asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table
Auditing Resource Group Organization with Resource Graph

Resource Graph's ResourceContainers table holds all resource groups, subscriptions, and management groups. Use it to find empty resource groups (cleanup candidates), subscriptions with excessive resource group counts (fragmentation), resource groups whose names do not match the naming convention, and resource groups that are over-loaded with too many resources.

// Resource Graph: audit resource group organization compliance

// 1. Resource groups with no resources (candidates for cleanup)
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | summarize ResourceCount = count() by resourceGroup
) on $left.name == $right.resourceGroup
| where isnull(ResourceCount) or ResourceCount == 0
| project name, subscriptionId, location, ResourceCount = coalesce(ResourceCount, 0)
| order by subscriptionId asc

// 2. Resources spread across too many resource groups (fragmentation check)
// Flag subscriptions with more than 50 resource groups (governance smell)
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| summarize RgCount = count() by subscriptionId
| where RgCount > 50
| order by RgCount desc

// 3. Resources not following the rg-{workload}-{layer}-{env}-{region} pattern
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| where name !matches regex @'^rg-[a-z0-9]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+$'
     and name !matches regex @'^rg-[a-z0-9]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+$'
| project name, subscriptionId, location
| order by subscriptionId asc

// 4. Distribution of resources per resource group (identify RGs that are too large)
Resources
| summarize ResourceCount = count() by resourceGroup, subscriptionId
| where ResourceCount > 200
| order by ResourceCount desc
Management Group Hierarchy Design

The Cloud Adoption Framework recommends a three-level management group hierarchy below the tenant root: platform (connectivity, identity, management subscriptions), workloads (production and non-production sub-trees), and sandbox. This structure lets you assign baseline security policies at the root, workload-specific policies at the workloads level, and relaxed development policies at the sandbox level — all without duplicating policy assignments per subscription.

# Management group hierarchy design and verification

# List the entire management group hierarchy
az account management-group list --query '[].{Id:id, Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}' -o table

# Show the full hierarchy tree (recursive)
az account management-group show \
  --name mg-corp \
  --expand \
  --recurse \
  --query 'children[].{Name:name, Type:type}' -o table

# Move a subscription between management groups
# (Policies from the target MG will apply to the subscription after the move)
az account management-group subscription remove \
  --name mg-workloads \
  --subscription '<subscription-id>'

az account management-group subscription add \
  --name mg-platform \
  --subscription '<subscription-id>'

# Create a nested management group structure
# Recommended CAF hierarchy:
#   Root
#   ├── mg-platform           (connectivity, identity, management)
#   │   ├── sub-connectivity-prod
#   │   └── sub-identity-prod
#   ├── mg-workloads          (production and pre-prod workload subscriptions)
#   │   ├── mg-prod
#   │   │   ├── sub-payments-prod
#   │   │   └── sub-catalog-prod
#   │   └── mg-nonprod
#   │       ├── sub-payments-dev
#   │       └── sub-catalog-dev
#   └── mg-sandbox            (developer experimentation)
#       └── sub-dev-sandbox

az account management-group create --name mg-prod     --display-name 'Production'     --parent mg-workloads
az account management-group create --name mg-nonprod  --display-name 'Non-Production'  --parent mg-workloads
Organization Anti-Patterns
Anti-pattern
Consequence
Better approach
One resource group for everything in a subscription
Cannot delete a single workload's resources; no lifecycle isolation; RBAC too broad
Per-workload or per-layer resource groups
Resource groups named after teams instead of workloads
Resources outlive team ownership changes; auditing by workload becomes impossible
Name by workload and environment: rg-payments-prod-eastus
All environments in one subscription
Blast radius: a policy or quota change affects prod and dev simultaneously
Separate subscriptions per environment (prod, nonprod) under different MGs
Flat management group hierarchy (no sub-groups)
Cannot apply different policies to prod vs dev subscriptions without per-subscription assignments
Use mg-prod / mg-nonprod children under the workloads MG
Manually creating resource groups via the portal
RGs drift from IaC state; no history of who created them or when
Create all RGs via Bicep or Terraform at subscription scope
Resources in the wrong resource group 'for convenience'
Cannot clean up by deleting the RG without manual resource extraction first
Respect lifecycle grouping: same group = same deployment and deletion cycle
Subscription Limits and Why They Drive Workload Isolation

Every Azure subscription carries per-region limits on vCPUs, storage accounts, VNets, public IPs, and other resource types. A single subscription that hosts all workloads will eventually hit these limits, and a quota increase request takes days. Isolating each major workload in its own subscription means limits are independent — a payments workload hitting its vCPU limit does not affect the catalog workload. Monitor current usage with az vm list-usage and request quota increases via support tickets before limits become blockers.

# Azure subscription resource limits — why subscription-per-workload matters

# Show current usage and limits for a subscription
az vm list-usage --location eastus \
  --query '[?localName=="Total Regional vCPUs"].{Name:localName, Current:currentValue, Limit:limit}' \
  -o table

# Show storage account limit (250 per subscription in most regions)
az resource list --resource-type 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts' \
  --query 'length(@)' -o tsv

# Request a quota increase via Support (programmatic)
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name 'vCPU-quota-increase-eastus' \
  --title 'Request vCPU quota increase in East US' \
  --description 'Need to increase Total Regional vCPUs from 100 to 500 in East US for prod payments workload' \
  --problem-classification '/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/quota-service-guid/problemClassifications/compute-vm-quota-guid' \
  --severity minimal \
  --contact-detail '{
    "firstName": "Platform",
    "lastName": "Team",
    "primaryEmailAddress": "platform-team@contoso.com",
    "preferredContactMethod": "Email",
    "preferredTimeZone": "UTC",
    "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US"
  }'

# Key subscription limits (as of 2026):
#   Total Regional vCPUs:          varies by region (default 10-350 depending on subscription age)
#   Storage accounts per region:   250
#   Resource groups per sub:       980
#   VNets per region:              50 (default, increasable)
#   Public IP addresses per region: 80 standard (default, increasable)
#   Key Vaults per sub:            100 per region

# Spreading workloads across subscriptions avoids hitting these limits
# and eliminates the blast radius if a limit is hit in one workload.
Summary

Azure resource organization uses four scope levels: management groups for cross-subscription Policy and RBAC, subscriptions as billing and blast-radius boundaries, resource groups as lifecycle and access-control units, and resources as the actual deployed services. The recommended pattern for production workloads is separate resource groups per layer (app, data, network) within each subscription, with subscriptions grouped in management groups that reflect the organization's structure. Create resource groups via IaC so they are tracked in state rather than managed manually.
